{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks whether Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS) can be associated with a mutation in the Super Oxide Dismutase 1 (SOD1) gene. Multiple documents confirm this association. Document [1] states that mutations in the SOD1 gene represent the second-most common cause of familial ALS, occurring in 10% to 20% of familial cases and 1% to 2% of sporadic cases. Document [2] explicitly states that the SOD1 gene encodes superoxide dismutase and that mutations in this gene are linked to ALS. Document [3] confirms that SOD1 mutations lead to a toxic gain of function in the protein, contributing to neurodegeneration. Document [10] and [12] emphasize that Tofersen, a drug approved for ALS, is specifically indicated for patients with a confirmed SOD1 mutation, targeting the mutant SOD1 mRNA and protein. Document [29] notes that mutations in SOD1 resulting in reduced function correlate with ALS development due to toxic superoxide accumulation. These consistent references across multiple sources establish a clear and direct association between SOD1 gene mutations and ALS. Therefore, the answer is definitively 'yes'.", "answer_choice": "A"}
